EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite effective bulk shear values of less than 25 knots, a mini-supercell thunderstorm developed in an airmass characterized by high surface dewpoints ahead of a tropical cyclone over the Gulf of Mexico. The storm produced an E1 tornado over western Anderson County. A few other reports of straight line wind damage and low end flooding also occurred with other storms on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey found the path of a weak tornado that began in a field east of Lake Hartwell, just west of highway 187 north of the Country Meadows intersection. The tornado traveled east northeast to a farmstead on highway 187, blowing away a small outbuilding and peeling some metal panels off the roof of a larger shed. The tornado continued ENE to Busby Rd, where a portion of the roof was removed from a home. The house also shifted slightly on its foundation while a deck was lifted and tossed and a garage door caved in. The track has lost at this point, as the tornado moved over open fields. However, the path was picked up again at Mar-Mac Rd, near the Martin Rd intersection, where numerous trees were uprooted or snapped. Some shingles were removed from a home and some outbuildings damaged in this areas we well. The end of the damage path was found near Tiffany Dr and Oak Noll Terrace, where two large pine trees were blown onto a home, causing substantial damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southerly flow developed within the warm sector ahead of a potent cold front. Wind gusts reached as high as 40-50 mph across parts of the area due to strong gradient winds well in advance of the cold front. A line of low topped convection with little to no lightning entered western portions of the forecast area shortly after 8 pm and moved off the coast by midnight, quickly followed by the cold front itself. Strong dynamical forcing accompanied the front, but surface based instability was near zero most of the evening. The low topped convection was supported by 0-6 km shear values of 60-80 kt along with favorable upper level divergence from a 150 kt jet. Mid level lapse rates were poor throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Colleton County 911 Center reported a tree down along Interstate 95, near mile marker 54.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low increased wind shear and instability across western South Carolina. Numerous thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon hours. Some of the storms became severe and a weak tornado was spawned over Greenville County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS survey team found the path of a weak tornado that began in a wooded area just northeast of Lauren Trace Circle. A few trees were downed at this location, with one tree taking down a power line. The tornado intensified as it moved east/northeast from there, downing multiple trees and snapping numerous large limbs along a creek bed before uprooting multiple large trees at a home site on Prince Lake. The tornado continued east northeast, snapping large limbs and knocking down a few trees as it crossed Old Gunter Rd and Mellyn St. About a half-dozen medium to large trees were uprooted at a home between Mellyn St and Mellwood Dr. The tornado lifted shortly after crossing Mellwood Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea, a minimal storm with sustained winds of 45 mph, moved up the coast of the Carolinas the morning of June 7th, bringing torrential rain, brief tropical storm force winds, and isolated severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea crossed Florida, scraped the coast of Georgia, and the center was near Hilton Head Island the morning of June 7th. Heavy rain preceded the storm, with tropical rain continuing throughout the day. Rainfall totals were impressive, with many spots receiving 3-4 inches. Brunswick county had the highest totals. An area east of Bolivia received 6.46 inches. There were numerous reports of street flooding. Some of the notables: There was a report of over two feet of water on Ridgemont Drive near Leland, NC. Running water was reported on Hwy 87 in Boiling Springs Lakes. A foot of water was also reported on Long Beach Road near the airport in Southport. The strongest winds came during and slightly after the center of Andrea past that afternoon. The Wilmington State Port clocked in at 43 kts, with 42 kts reported at the Wilmington International Airport. North Topsail also came in at 43 kts. A 30 ft Grady White Vessel was blown off its lift at the dock at Wrightsville Beach. In Horry County, a large tree fell on a mobile home on Wiley Drive. Unfortunately, there appears to be on fatality due to the storm. A surfer went in the water at 42nd Ave in Cherry Grove and was never found.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through SC Saturday Feb. 16 producing rain which changed to snow in the northern Midlands and upper Pee Dee area.  Most areas received around an inch of snow but some isolated areas in Lancaster, Kershaw, and Lee counties received 1 to 3 inches of snow in thunder snow showers.  The convective band of snow was generally along and just west of SC Hwy 341 from Heath Springs to Bishopville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported around an inch of snow in northern, central, and eastern Fairfield county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and very moist environment was present over the southeastern United States ahead of a cold front to the west. Strong low level winds with 0-6km shear between 50-60 knots as well as PWATS near 1.8 inches and MLCAPE between 500-600 J/kg supported a few severe thunderstorms within the warm sector ahead of the cold front before it swept offshore by early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Berkeley County 911 call center reported a tree down at the 2200 block of Santee River Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey confirmed that straight line wind damage occurred with winds estimated between 65 and 75 miles per hour. A few homes had siding ripped off and minor shingle damage along Sandy Pointe Lane. A front porch also had minor damage on one home. Also, palm trees were damaged along the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light sleet developed across much of the western Carolinas and northeast Georgia during the morning. The intermittent sleet eventually changed to light freezing rain in most areas by late afternoon. Most areas north of the I-85 corridor saw measurable sleet, generally less than a quarter inch. A light glaze then fell on top of that, making for treacherous driving conditions during the afternoon. Most areas south of the I-85 corridor saw only trace accumulations, but that was enough to cause plenty of slick spots. Hundreds of accidents were reported across the area, especially along the I-85 corridor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southerly flow developed within the warm sector ahead of a potent cold front. Wind gusts reached as high as 40-50 mph across parts of the area due to strong gradient winds well in advance of the cold front. A line of low topped convection with little to no lightning entered western portions of the forecast area shortly after 8 pm and moved off the coast by midnight, quickly followed by the cold front itself. Strong dynamical forcing accompanied the front, but surface based instability was near zero most of the evening. The low topped convection was supported by 0-6 km shear values of 60-80 kt along with favorable upper level divergence from a 150 kt jet. Mid level lapse rates were poor throughout the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light sleet developed across much of the western Carolinas and northeast Georgia during the morning. The intermittent sleet eventually changed to light freezing rain in most areas by late afternoon. Most areas north of the I-85 corridor saw measurable sleet, generally less than a quarter inch. A light glaze then fell on top of that, making for treacherous driving conditions during the afternoon. Most areas south of the I-85 corridor saw only trace accumulations, but that was enough to cause plenty of slick spots. Hundreds of accidents were reported across the area, especially along the I-85 corridor.

A 16 year male was found drowned later on July 14; a 38 year old male was later found drowned on July 15; the third body of a 17 year old male was found July 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous to widespread thunderstorms developed over the area as several mid level shortwaves ejected northeast over a stationary front between an inland upper trough and high pressure offshore. Tropical air featuring PWATs over 2 inches and repeating thunderstorms produced torrential rain and several reports of flash flooding across the Charleston Tri-county region. A couple of thunderstorms became severe with damaging wind gusts and heavy rainfall during the afternoon. Two storms exhibited sufficient rotation to prompt Tornado Warnings.  Persistent onshore winds and long period swell also led to a moderate risk of rip currents along the coast.  One rip current resulted in a drowning while an individual tried to rescue another person already in a rip current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person drowned trying to rescue another person in a rip current.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upstream shortwave, a seabreeze pinned along the coast and outflow boundaries supported numerous thunderstorms within an environment characterized by MLCAPE exceeding 3000 j/kg and downdraft CAPE ranging between 800-1200 j/kg. Two thunderstorms impacted the Charleston Tri-County region while another small complex of thunderstorms moved into Allendale, Hampton and Colleton Counties during the evening. Most reports included damaging winds, where brief wet microbursts blew down trees and power lines at scattered locations. The largest hail was limited to quarter size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported a pine tree down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea lifted northeast out of the Gulf of Mexico Thursday night and over southeast Georgia and southeast South Carolina into Friday. Periods of heavy rain and damaging wind gusts occurred with showers and thunderstorms associated with the tropical system as it passed over the area and eventually to the northeastern United States.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jasper County 911 call center reported a tree down along Interstate 95 near mile marker 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Initially, multi-cell thunderstorms developed inland within an unstable environment featuring MLCAPES between 2500-3500 j/kg and a well defined shortwave trough just north/northwest of the forecast area. These thunderstorms shifted south/southeast toward the coast, prompting warnings for inland counties, while other thunderstorms developed near the seabreeze pinned at the coast. Eventually, complex mesoscale boundaries developed and dominated convective initiation as well as the growth and propagation of thunderstorms, supporting occasional episodes of severe weather especially within coastal counties from Charleston to McIntosh. The most intense thunderstorm produced tennis ball size hail in the Shadowmoss subdivision in West Ashley, while golf ball size hail fell in Meridian in McIntosh County. We also received other reports of quarter size hail and wind damage. Two storms in western Charleston County encountered boundaries and began to rotate, prompting Tornado Warnings. Almost 6 inches of rain fell in a small area on James Island and over 4 inches of rain fell across a swath of Beaufort County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported penny size hail at Tanner Plantation in Hanahan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upstream shortwave, a seabreeze pinned along the coast and outflow boundaries supported numerous thunderstorms within an environment characterized by MLCAPE exceeding 3000 j/kg and downdraft CAPE ranging between 800-1200 j/kg. Two thunderstorms impacted the Charleston Tri-County region while another small complex of thunderstorms moved into Allendale, Hampton and Colleton Counties during the evening. Most reports included damaging winds, where brief wet microbursts blew down trees and power lines at scattered locations. The largest hail was limited to quarter size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sub severe winds estimated between 40 to 50 mph knocked down a diseased tree onto the awning of a pharmacy store.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous to widespread thunderstorms developed over the area as several mid level shortwaves ejected northeast over a stationary front between an inland upper trough and high pressure offshore. Tropical air featuring PWATs over 2 inches and repeating thunderstorms produced torrential rain and several reports of flash flooding across the Charleston Tri-county region. A couple of thunderstorms became severe with damaging wind gusts and heavy rainfall during the afternoon. Two storms exhibited sufficient rotation to prompt Tornado Warnings.  Persistent onshore winds and long period swell also led to a moderate risk of rip currents along the coast.  One rip current resulted in a drowning while an individual tried to rescue another person already in a rip current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser reported Ashley Avenue impassable due to high water on the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and strong surface heating set up an environment favorable for strong to severe thunderstorms as a cold front stalled over the region and interacted with an inland moving seabreeze.  A few storms showed strong rotation with one eventually producing a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS survey team determined a tornado touched down just north of the Duncan Chapel Cemetery near Duncan Chapel Road. The tornado moved south|southeast while crossing Interstate 95 just south of the Duncan Chapel|Road Bridge. The tornado crossed a number of fields and forested areas, including a livestock field before lifting in an open area off Quail Ridge Road. The tornado damaged corn crops and trees along its path. No significant structures were known have been damaged. Based on the level and type of damage observed, the tornado was rated ef-1 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale with maximum winds of 85 to 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Initially, multi-cell thunderstorms developed inland within an unstable environment featuring MLCAPES between 2500-3500 j/kg and a well defined shortwave trough just north/northwest of the forecast area. These thunderstorms shifted south/southeast toward the coast, prompting warnings for inland counties, while other thunderstorms developed near the seabreeze pinned at the coast. Eventually, complex mesoscale boundaries developed and dominated convective initiation as well as the growth and propagation of thunderstorms, supporting occasional episodes of severe weather especially within coastal counties from Charleston to McIntosh. The most intense thunderstorm produced tennis ball size hail in the Shadowmoss subdivision in West Ashley, while golf ball size hail fell in Meridian in McIntosh County. We also received other reports of quarter size hail and wind damage. Two storms in western Charleston County encountered boundaries and began to rotate, prompting Tornado Warnings. Almost 6 inches of rain fell in a small area on James Island and over 4 inches of rain fell across a swath of Beaufort County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The media reported a tree and power line down on Riverland Drive.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey team determined a tornado initially touched down 5 miles east of Bowman, South Carolina and traveled southeast out of Orangeburg County and into northern Dorchester County. The tornado crossed Interstate 95 just north of the Duncan Chapel Road Bridge and continued southeast across Pinebreak Road, Weathers Farm Road and Hinkle Road before lifting near the intersection of U.S. Highway 15 and Duncan Chapel Road. The tornado damaged corn crops and numerous trees along its path. One house sustained minor roof|damage and a tree fell through a trailer. Based on the level and type of damage observed, the tornado was rated an ef-1 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale with maximum winds of 100 to 110 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upstream shortwave, a seabreeze pinned along the coast and outflow boundaries supported numerous thunderstorms within an environment characterized by MLCAPE exceeding 3000 j/kg and downdraft CAPE ranging between 800-1200 j/kg. Two thunderstorms impacted the Charleston Tri-County region while another small complex of thunderstorms moved into Allendale, Hampton and Colleton Counties during the evening. Most reports included damaging winds, where brief wet microbursts blew down trees and power lines at scattered locations. The largest hail was limited to quarter size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated as a mid level shortwave shifted over deep moisture in the southeastern United States. Differential heating boundaries from previous cloud cover provided a focusing mechanism for a few thunderstorms to become severe with damaging wind gusts and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported stalled cars due to flooding on Rutledge Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Initially, multi-cell thunderstorms developed inland within an unstable environment featuring MLCAPES between 2500-3500 j/kg and a well defined shortwave trough just north/northwest of the forecast area. These thunderstorms shifted south/southeast toward the coast, prompting warnings for inland counties, while other thunderstorms developed near the seabreeze pinned at the coast. Eventually, complex mesoscale boundaries developed and dominated convective initiation as well as the growth and propagation of thunderstorms, supporting occasional episodes of severe weather especially within coastal counties from Charleston to McIntosh. The most intense thunderstorm produced tennis ball size hail in the Shadowmoss subdivision in West Ashley, while golf ball size hail fell in Meridian in McIntosh County. We also received other reports of quarter size hail and wind damage. Two storms in western Charleston County encountered boundaries and began to rotate, prompting Tornado Warnings. Almost 6 inches of rain fell in a small area on James Island and over 4 inches of rain fell across a swath of Beaufort County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a tree down on a house along Bishop Drive in West Ashley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds aloft were in place well ahead of a cold front. There were many reports of gradient wind damage through the day and into the night. A line of forced convection developed along the front and swept across the area during the late night hours. The convection slightly enhanced the strong winds and resulted in additional reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down along Ashland Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The triple point of a low pressure system passed just to the northwest of the area and helped to drag a cold front across the Pee Dee Region of South Carolina during the evening. A small, elongated cluster of low-topped showers and thunderstorms formed along the front as it began moving across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down along a right of way between Ebenezer Road and Hoffmeyer Road. Some of the trees were reportedly rotted near their bases. The tops of a couple healthy pine trees were snapped. Some of the trees fell across large power transmission lines causing serious damage and a significant power outage. At the peak of the outage, there were reportedly 14,000 people without power. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms ahead of an approaching cold front started developing and intensifying in the mid to late afternoon hours across northern Georgia and Alabama. There were also discrete supercells developing ahead of the main line during peak heating. The line approached the western part of the forecast area around 9 pm as the line was beginning to interact and consolidate with the discrete cells out ahead of it. The line swept across the entire forecast area with numerous reports of wind damage and a smaller collection of large hail. The near storm environment was marked by impressive mid levels with 500-700 mb lapse rates less than -7 C/km and LI's as low as -3 to -4 C. The presence of an elevated mixed layer resulted in DCAPE values in the 800-1000 J/kg range. The upstream convective line was forced by strong ambient shear (on the order of 50-60 kts in the 0-6 km layer) with adequate surface based instability (CAPE in the 500-750 J/kg range). The supercell elements upstream yielded a tornado threat which subsequently diminished into the forecast area with less instability present and a convective mode favoring linear segments.  The Storm Prediction Center hoisted a Severe Thunderstorm Watch around 7:30 pm for all of southeast Georgia and the portion of South Carolina that bordered the Savannah river. The line not only maintained its strength but intensified with base velocities upwards of 70 knots at times as it entered the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter size hail in Scotia, South Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms took down trees and powerlines across the Midlands and Pee Dee regions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public and Emergency Manager reported numerous trees down around the Dalzell area. The Public reported 47 trees either downed or leaning over in the Kel Sam Farms Subdivision in Dalzell.

The line swept across the entire forecast area with numerous reports of wind damage and a smaller collection of large hail. The near storm environment was marked by impressive mid levels with 500-700 mb lapse rates less than -7 C/km and LI's as low as -3 to -4 C. The presence of an elevated mixed layer resulted in DCAPE values in the 800-1000 J/kg range. The upstream convective line was forced by strong ambient shear (on the order of 50-60 kts in the 0-6 km layer) with adequate surface based instability (CAPE in the 500-750 J/kg range). The supercell elements upstream yielded a tornado threat which subsequently diminished into the forecast area with less instability present and a convective mode favoring linear segments.  The Storm Prediction Center hoisted a Severe Thunderstorm Watch around 7:30 pm for all of southeast Georgia and the portion of South Carolina that bordered the Savannah river. The line not only maintained its strength but intensified with base velocities upwards of 70 knots at times as it entered the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colleton County 911 Call Center reported a tree down between the 4000 and 5000 block of Combahee Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm in eastern Orangeburg county produced an intense macroburst and EF1 tornado that destroyed a chicken barn and damaged several homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey found numerous trees down along and south of I-20 from mile marker 161 to I-95.  I-20 was closed due to trees across the interstate.  A chicken barn was destroyed near the intersection of Winter Creek and Funderburg roads and field irrigation systems were rolled over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over land along with deepening low pressure off the Atlantic coast set up a strong pressure gradient over the southeastern United States. Cold air associated with the high allowed 1000 mb geostrophic winds of 40 to 50 knots to mix to the surface and produce a period of wind advisory conditions over a small area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The media reported a tree down near the intersection of Green Pond Highway and Locust Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of widespread light to moderate rain moved across much of southeast South Carolina and southeast Georgia during the overnight and early morning hours.  Increased rainfall rates within embedded thunderstorms caused excessive amounts and eventually flash flooding around Beaufort.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The media reported the entrance ramp to Highway 170 and Highway 278 closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the central Carolinas ahead of an approaching cold front. These thunderstorms moved into a maritime tropical air-mass with dewpoints near 70 degrees and temperatures well up in the 80s. The environment was rather strongly sheared, 0-6 km bulk shear was greater than 40 knots and unstable, surface-based CAPE values around 1500 J/kg. Discrete cells organized into a Quasi-Linear Convective System, resulting in wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several gage points along rivers in SC went above flood stage due to 2-4 inches of rainfall.  Most flooding was confined to low lying areas but some areas had some minor flooding issues with recreation and other areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Congaree National Park reported flooding of several areas within the park closing areas to tourists. The lower boardwalk was covered with several feet of water and portions of the upper boardwalk near Lake Weston even became submerged with several inches of water. These thunderstorms moved into a maritime tropical air-mass with dewpoints near 70 degrees and temperatures well up in the 80s. The environment was rather strongly sheared, 0-6 km bulk shear was greater than 40 knots and unstable, surface-based CAPE values around 1500 J/kg. Discrete cells organized into a Quasi-Linear Convective System, resulting in wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were reported down.

One fire fighter went into cardiac arrest but was revived and is doing well.  At least 100 people were displaced, and the Red Cross set up a shelter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Modest instability and an approaching weak shortwave helped support a cluster of thunderstorms across coastal Horry County. As the storms encountered stronger south winds near the beaches and increased low-level vorticity, a tornadic circulation developed southwest of Socastee and moved north several miles to near Highway 31. The circulation then weakened, but continued moving north as a funnel cloud before dissipating northwest of Myrtle Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team concluded an EF0 tornado briefly touched down near the intersection of Highway 31 and Highway 544 near the retention pond off of Breezewood Boulevard in Socastee, South Carolina. The maximum wind speed was estimated to 60 mph. The path length was slightly less than a tenth of a mile and the maximum path width was 50 yards.  The weak tornado caused mainly minor damage to approximately 4 homes. Damage was limited primarily to the loss of a small number of shingles, the loss of small amounts of vinyl siding and soffit trim. A few downed trees were also observed. The monetary damage estimate is $15,000 dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Modest instability and an approaching weak shortwave helped support a cluster of thunderstorms across coastal Horry County. As the storms encountered stronger south winds near the beaches and increased low-level vorticity, a tornadic circulation developed southwest of Socastee and moved north several miles to near Highway 31. The circulation then weakened, but continued moving north as a funnel cloud before dissipating northwest of Myrtle Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team concluded an EF0 tornado briefly touched down in the Cameron Village subdivision off of Bay Road, between Highway 707 and Enterprise Road in Socastee, South Carolina. The maximum wind speed was estimated to 60 mph. The path length was just over a tenth of a mile and the maximum path width was 30 yards. Significant damage occurred to the framing of a two-story house under construction. The framing of the first floor collapsed and construction debris became airborne and caused moderate damage to the house next door to include broken windows. Otherwise the weak tornado caused mainly minor damage to approximately 6 homes. Damage was limited primarily to the loss of a small number of shingles, the loss of small amounts of vinyl siding, soffit trim, fencing, patio furniture and an air compressor. The monetary damage estimate is $35,000 dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rich tongue of 70-plus dewpoints ahead of an approaching shortwave and surface cold front as well as a strengthening upper and lower level jet set up favorable conditions for a few stronger thunderstorms along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey team found straight line wind damage with winds estimated between 60 to 70 mph. Two palmetto trees were snapped. One four inch oak limb was also snapped. A pool awning collapsed and a trampoline was tossed 300 feet.  A weather vane was blown off the top of a barn roof. A kids plastic play house was broken and a patio table and chairs were blown 30 feet and broken. No damage was seen to the primary house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved across Southeast South Carolina starting in the afternoon and continuing through the overnight hours. Strong winds developed in associated with the strong pressure gradient and the presence of a strong low level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts have knocked down several trees across the county. The trees have brought down power lines which has resulted in some power outages and a few small fires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved across Southeast South Carolina starting in the afternoon and continuing through the overnight hours. Strong winds developed in associated with the strong pressure gradient and the presence of a strong low level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio reported trees down on Fish Road near Highway 27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong rip currents caused the drowning of two people at Myrtle Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong rip currents pulled two people out to sea.  A teen got caught in a rip current near 48th Ave N, and another man who tried to rescue the boy was also pulled out from the rip current.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent southeast wind and swell produced strong rip currents at North Myrtle Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong rip currents caused a man to drown at 15th Avenue South in Myrtle Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced very heavy rain over western Upstate South Carolina, triggering areas of flash flooding. One fatality resulted from a car being swept into a flooded stream. An automated rain gauge 4 miles west of Clemson recorded 3.83 inches of rain in around a two hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clamp Drive was flooded near Hencoop Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough embedded within an elongated trough of low pressure initiated a line of thunderstorms over the southeastern United States.  Several thunderstorms along the line became strong as they entered an environment characterized by surface based cape values near 4000 J/KG and lifted index values between -7 to -8 C.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported nickel size hail in Park West near the Mount Pleasant Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell moved north with no reports of damage until it spawned a tornado 2 miles east of Quinby in Florence County, South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team concluded an EF1 tornado developed near the South Carolina Armory between South Stadium Road and South Greer Road. The tornado snapped a large tree and limbs were down in this location. The tornado then moved north and across Highway 76 and then across East Sammys Lane, snapping trees and downing tree limbs along its path. A couple homes were damaged in the Battery Park subdivision. One home had approximately 30 percent of its roof structure removed. Trees were down across the subdivision. The tornado continued north and nearly parallel to Williamson Road causing dozens of snapped trees and large limbs to block a large section of the roadway. After crossing Williamson Road, the tornado downed and snapped trees in a stand in the middle of a large field. Then it entered a mobile home park on the south side of East Old Marion Highway, west of Highway 327. The tornado destroyed 10 mobile homes and 2 other homes sustained major damage. In addition, there were 18 mobile homes that sustained minor damage. Nine people sustained minor injuries and were transported to the hospital, two of which were actually trapped in their homes and had to be extricated. The tornado exited the mobile home park and crossed East Old Marion Highway, snapping more trees and damaging a section of fence along the road. The tornado dissipated on the north side of East Old Marion Highway prior to reaching Highway 327. The maximum wind speed was estimated to 105 mph. The maximum path width was 90 yards. The tornado covered a little more than 3 miles in approximately 11 minutes. The monetary damage estimate is $87,979 dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains over the Midlands and Pee Dee regions of South Carolina produced some flash flooding that closed several roads around the area and flooding low lying areas with 1 to 2 feet of water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Patrol reported Wire road closed between Juniper Springs and Taylor Street in Gilbert.  Other rural roads that were closed included Martin Smith, Sarah Ann, and Neely Wingard due to high water covering the roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains over the Midlands and Pee Dee regions of South Carolina produced some flash flooding that closed several roads around the area and flooding low lying areas with 1 to 2 feet of water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported several roads closed between Hwys 265 and 109 due to flash flooding from heavy rains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front slowly lifted north over portions of southeast South Carolina during early morning hours.  Deep moisture accompanied the front which helped set up an environment capable of producing heavy rain and flash flooding as showers and thunderstorms trained over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Charleston County 911 Call Center reported portions of Dorchester Road closed between Meeting Street and Pinehaven Drive due to water on the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface trough and mid level shortwave provided the main forcing mechanism for showers and thunderstorms over inland areas. A few of the thunderstorms became strong, producing damaging wind gusts and heavy rainfall at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter size hail along the 100 block of Middleton Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated as a mid level shortwave shifted over deep moisture in the southeastern United States. Differential heating boundaries from previous cloud cover provided a focusing mechanism for a few thunderstorms to become severe with damaging wind gusts and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Carolina Highway Patrol reported a tree down on Dixie Plantation Road near Highway 162.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced large hail over Spartanburg County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail was reported at the intersection of Sugar Ridge Rd and Clark Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving line of thunderstorms crossed all of western South Carolina during the late afternoon and evening hours. The storms developed along a strong cold front. Despite their slow movement, the storms were able to mix down strong environmental winds, causing scattered areas of straight line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Fant Lane and another at Lakeview Heights and Pineneedle Dr in Union. A power pole was also blown over on Park Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe Pulse Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front and produced some wind damage and 1 inch hail over areas of the Midlands, CSRA, and Pee Dee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 1 inch hail fell for about 5 minutes in the Pelion area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached from the west, supported by a potent shortwave. Temperatures in the mid 80s helped increase instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported trees in the roadway near the intersection of Lumber road and Cutler road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a cold front and moved into an air-mass that remained moderately unstable even as drier air worked into the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on a power line along Highway 261 near the Stuckey Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms over central South Carolina and the Savannah River Area produced some wind damage taking down trees and a few powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Savannah River Site measured a 63 MPH wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produce wind damage around the Midlands of South Carolina taking down trees and also produced street flooding and several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Augusta TV station reported trees down in Williston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front over the region with precipitable water values near 2 inches provided ample moisture in conjunction with the sea breeze. Numerous thunderstorms popped up in the afternoon, producing heavy rain over a short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several road closures were reported closed due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a stationary front over the North Carolina Blue Ridge. As the storms moved into the Upstate, a few reports of severe weather were received early in the day, with the event transitioning to a flash flood mode by the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of gusty winds and extremely heavy rainfall caused a tree to fall on and destroy a home on East Main St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms dropped very heavy rain over the northeast piedmont of South Carolina, resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mount Lebanon Road between Hully Bridge Road and Putnam Road was closed due to flooding. A road closure was reported at the intersection of West Springs Highway and Sulphur Springs Road (4 WSW Jonesville).  Dillard Road from Wildcat Road to the Spartanburg County line was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a trough inland and High Pressure to the east, thunderstorms formed along the seasbreeze during the late afternoon. Some storms reached severe criteria and also produced heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Patrol reported the road closed due to flooding at the intersection of Savannah Round and Old Trolley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms over central South Carolina and the Savannah River Area produced some wind damage taking down trees and a few powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Columbia Police Dept. reported flooding in areas around Two Notch Road around the 9000 block.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak wave of Low Pressure and a mid level trough supported widespread convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warning Point reported a tree down on powerlines near the intersection of Freedom Parkway and Ford Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced very heavy rain over western Upstate South Carolina, triggering areas of flash flooding. One fatality resulted from a car being swept into a flooded stream. An automated rain gauge 4 miles west of Clemson recorded 3.83 inches of rain in around a two hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Furman L Smith Memorial Highway was flooded over a 40 foot stretch near the intersection of Blue Bird Lane. Six Mile Creek flooded Jones Mill Road. The bridge over Jones Mill Creek was damaged and partially collapsed by flood borne debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall rates of 2 per hour produced 2 to 5 inches of rain over Columbia causing flash flooding in many areas.  Several roads had to be closed and water rose to 4 foot depths in some locations such as Main and Whaley Streets.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: THE USGS gauge along Rocky Branch Creek at the intersection of Main and Whaley Streets rose to 11.02 feet.  Flood stage is 7.2 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary produce thunderstorms with heavy rain and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at the intersection of 76th Ave North and North Kings Hwy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong squall lined moved through the state producing high winds taking down trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported a few trees down on powerlines in the southwest side of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms dropped very heavy rain on the communities south of Greenville, resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain fell across the ���Golden Strip��� of Greenville County, including the communities of Mauldin, Simpsonville and the Five Forks area. Rainfall amounts of 3 to 4 inches were common in this area. Severe urban flooding developed with water entering some businesses and private residences. Several roads were flooded and closed, including Holland Rd which was flooded by Bridge Fork Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing thunderstorms due to a stalled front over the region with precipitable water values near 2 inches continued throughout the night, producing heavy rain over a short period of time. In Allendale County, a dam break occurred on private pond, sending a massive amount of trees/debris over roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Concord Church Road between Highway 301 and Gadson Morris Road was closed due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front advanced over the southeastern United States and interacted with numerous boundaries over the area. Activity consolidated into a QLCS which swept across much of the county warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Carolina Department of Highways reported a tree down near the intersection of Highway 78 and Jedburg Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed for a second day over Upstate South Carolina in an airmass characterized by weak shear and moderate instability. Some of the storms produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell about 3 miles south of Pauline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small tornado briefly touched down in Fairfield County just northeast of Ridgeway taking down trees and damaging a few homes and cars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Patrol reported trees down on US 378 and Callison Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low increased wind shear and instability across western South Carolina. Numerous thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon hours. Some of the storms became severe and a weak tornado was spawned over Greenville County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Belton-Honea Path Highway, 2 miles northwest of Honea Path, and at the intersection of highways 178 and 76 in Honea Path. Other trees|were blown down on Firetower Rd and Austin Rd, both about 4 miles west Honea Path.
